summary:
Nemrik (also written Nemrik 9) is a Pre-Pottery Neolithic site in the Sinjar-Tigris region of northern Iraq, near the Tigris River, dated to approximately 10,000–8,600 BCE. Excavated by a Polish archaeological mission before the flooding of the Mosul Dam reservoir, Nemrik is one of the few well-documented early Neolithic sites in northern Mesopotamia and provides important evidence for the cultural traditions of this region at the dawn of the agricultural period.

Nemrik's architecture includes a sequence of round and oval structures transitioning to rectangular buildings, paralleling architectural development documented at other PPNA sites in the broader upper Mesopotamian cultural zone. The site has yielded evidence for the consumption of wild cereals and early cultivation, alongside hunting of aurochs, gazelle, and other game. Nemrik's position in northern Iraq places it at the interface of the Levantine, Anatolian, and Zagros cultural spheres, and its material culture shows connections to contemporaneous sites in [[event:3|southeastern Anatolia]] and the upper Euphrates valley. The site's excavation before its inundation was one of several important salvage archaeology operations in the region that secured data from sites that would otherwise have been lost to reservoir flooding.
